summary: The project has resolved 81 of 118 tracked issues (69%), with all remaining 37 open issues concentrated in just three problems: first_proof_1/q4 (22), q5 (14), and q6 (1). Most problem entries are fully resolved, but solvability scores remain low across the board (median ~16%), indicating that even closed issues may rest on fragile proofs. Resource usage today was minimal (2 runs, $0.19), suggesting capacity is available to push on the stuck problems.
problems: first_proof_1/q4 has 22 open issues — the single largest blocker, with only a 16% solvability score
first_proof_1/q5 has 14 open issues and the second-lowest solvability score (14%)
first_proof_1/q8 has the lowest solvability score (9%) despite being marked resolved, indicating a likely fragile or incorrect proof
Solvability scores are broadly low (q1–q6, q8–q9 all under 20%), suggesting systemic proof-quality risk even where issues are closed
Only 2 runs executed today — throughput is low relative to the open-issue backlog
All 10 best proofs use a single model (claude_opus_5-4), creating a model-diversity/single-point-of-failure risk
highlights: 81 of 118 issues resolved (~69% completion) with zero items stuck in-progress, indicating a clean pipeline
17 of 20 problem entries are fully resolved with no open issues
q7 (58%) and q10 (57%) have strong solvability scores and are the closest to being confidently solved
Cost efficiency is high today — only $0.1948 spent across 2 runs
All AIM problem lists and Erdős problems are fully resolved

suggested_todos.title: Allocate focused runs to clear the 22 open issues in first_proof_1/q4
Re-verify q8's resolved proof given its 9% solvability score
Push remaining 14 open issues in first_proof_1/q5
Close out the single remaining open issue in first_proof_1/q6
Run independent verification with an alternate model to reduce single-model dependence
Audit low-solvability resolved problems (q1-q6, q9) for proof fragility
Increase daily run throughput to match the open-issue backlog
